question: what happens if you have no good bacteria in your stomach
title: What Happens If You Have No Good Bacteria in Your Stomach? More Than You'd Think
metaDescription: Your gut bacteria isn't just a nice-to-have. Without good bacteria, digestion, immunity, mental health, and nutrient absorption all suffer. Here's what the science says, and what living food can do about it.
content:
# What Happens If You Have No Good Bacteria in Your Stomach? More Than You'd Think

Hey everybody. Here's a concept that took me a while to really land.

Your gut bacteria is not you. It's a separate organism, or more accurately a community of organisms, living inside you. You have a symbiotic relationship with it. It does things for you that you cannot do for yourself. And when that relationship breaks down, when the good bacteria disappear, you start falling apart in ways that seem completely unrelated to your digestion.

This isn't just about stomach aches. The consequences of a depleted gut microbiome touch virtually every system in your body. Here's what actually happens.

## Your Gut Is a Separate Living Thing

Inside your gastrointestinal tract lives a community of microorganisms, bacteria, fungi, viruses, and other tiny life forms, collectively called your gut microbiome. In a healthy adult, this community contains trillions of individual organisms representing hundreds of different species.

This is not you. These are not your cells. They have their own DNA, their own metabolism, their own needs. And yet they are so integrated into your functioning that without them, you would not be able to survive. They digest things you can't digest. They produce vitamins you can't produce on your own. They train your immune system. They communicate with your brain.

The National Institutes of Health has spent enormous resources mapping the human microbiome precisely because of how central it is to health. What's become clear is that gut bacteria diversity is one of the most important predictors of overall health we have. A rich, diverse microbiome predicts resilience. A depleted, low-diversity microbiome predicts disease.

## Digestion Breaks Down Without Good Bacteria

The most immediate consequence of losing good gut bacteria is digestive dysfunction. Your gut bacteria are directly responsible for breaking down certain types of food, particularly the complex carbohydrates and dietary fibers that your own digestive enzymes can't touch. When those bacteria are gone, that food either sits undigested, causing bloating and discomfort, or it feeds the wrong organisms.

Cleveland Clinic describes the result as dysbiosis, an imbalance in the gut microbial community where harmful organisms outnumber the beneficial ones. The symptoms are familiar: bloating, gas, constipation, diarrhea, acid reflux, irritable bowel syndrome. These aren't just inconveniences. They're signals that the digestive system has lost the biological support it needs to function properly.

I've talked about this at presentations at the Houston Botanical Garden, and people are always surprised to learn that probiotic supplements face a real challenge getting good bacteria to the colon where they're needed. Your stomach is an acid bath, around pH 4 or lower, that neutralizes a lot of bacteria before they ever reach the intestines. This is actually one of the reasons I'm more interested in cultivating good bacteria through food than trying to bomb-drop a probiotic capsule past the stomach.

## Nutrient Absorption Suffers

Here's one that surprises people. Even if you're eating nutritious food, a depleted microbiome means you're not absorbing the full value of that food. Your gut bacteria are part of the nutrient delivery system, they break down compounds and make nutrients bioavailable in forms your body can actually use.

Gut bacteria synthesize vitamins, including vitamin K and several B vitamins, right there in your intestinal tract. They break down polyphenols from plant foods into forms your body can absorb. They regulate how your body processes and stores fat. They affect insulin sensitivity and blood sugar regulation.

When I talk about "living calories" from garden food, this is part of what I mean. The beneficial bacteria on the surface of raw vegetables and fermented foods interact with your gut microbiome when you eat them. They're not just passengers, they're strengthening your gut's bacterial community with each exposure. Food grown in living soil and eaten fresh is teeming with that biological input. Processed food stripped of all bacteria delivers calories to a gut community that's getting no reinforcement.

## Your Immune System Loses Its Teacher

About 70 to 80 percent of your immune system is located in or adjacent to your gut. That's not an accident. Your gut bacteria are in constant conversation with your immune cells, basically teaching the immune system what to attack and what to leave alone.

Research published in PMC from the National Institutes of Health makes clear that gut bacteria are involved in immune training from the earliest days of life. A baby delivered through the birth canal is immediately colonized with beneficial bacteria from the mother. Those early bacterial colonizers train the infant's immune system to distinguish between friend and foe. When that process is disrupted, by C-sections, formula instead of breast milk, or early antibiotic use, the immune system doesn't learn properly.

In adults, depleting the good bacteria through chronic antibiotic use, processed food diets, or high stress leaves the immune system without its training partner. The result can be an immune system that either under-responds to actual threats or over-responds to harmless things, which is what you see in autoimmune disease and chronic inflammation.

Some research has connected specific losses of gut bacterial diversity to conditions like rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, type 1 diabetes, and inflammatory bowel disease. These are not gut diseases in the traditional sense. But they appear to have roots in a disrupted gut ecosystem.

## Mental Health and Sleep Connections

This is the one that blows people's minds. The gut produces about 90% of the body's serotonin. Serotonin is a neurotransmitter that affects mood, sleep, appetite, and cognitive function. And gut bacteria are directly involved in the production of serotonin and other neurotransmitters.

When good gut bacteria decline, serotonin production can be affected. The result shows up not just as digestive symptoms but as mood disorders, anxiety, depression, and sleep disruption. Researchers at WebMD and elsewhere have documented the connection between gut dysbiosis and mental health problems, though the mechanisms are still being worked out in detail.

I'm not saying gut bacteria imbalance is the sole cause of depression. But when you see someone with chronic anxiety, chronic fatigue, poor sleep, and digestive problems all at once, that pattern has gut microbiome written all over it. Address the gut, and you address a significant driver of that whole cluster of symptoms.

## How to Rebuild Good Gut Bacteria

Here's the practical piece. You can't just take a probiotic pill and fix this, though a good probiotic can help at the margins. What you need is a sustained feeding of the beneficial bacterial community over time. That's a dietary strategy, not a supplementation strategy.

Raw fruits and vegetables are living foods. They have bacteria on their surfaces, beneficial bacteria from the environment they grew in. When you eat them, those bacteria travel through your digestive tract and reinforce the colonies in your gut. This is one of the most important arguments for fresh produce over processed food. Pasteurized, processed, and packaged foods have had their bacteria killed. They don't make this contribution.

Fermented vegetables, sauerkraut, kimchi, fermented pickles, fermented hot sauce, are raw vegetables where the beneficial bacteria have been actively cultivated through the lacto-fermentation process. They're teeming with life in a way that even fresh vegetables aren't. That bacterial life has a better chance of surviving stomach transit and reaching the colon where it does its most important work, because it's housed in an acid-tolerant food matrix.

Dietary fiber from vegetables, legumes, and whole grains feeds the beneficial bacteria that are already there. This is prebiotic nutrition, you're feeding your existing bacterial community, helping it grow and crowd out the harmful organisms.

Y'all, I believe deeply that the health of your gut microbiome is the foundation of your health overall. And the path to building it runs through the same place the path to building healthy soil does, through the decay cycle, through biology, through living food from living ground. That connection is not an accident. We are, in the most literal sense, an extension of the ecology we eat from.

---

## Sources

- [Impacts of Gut Bacteria on Human Health and Diseases, PMC / NCBI](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4425030/)
- [What Is Your Gut Microbiome?, Cleveland Clinic](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/body/25201-gut-microbiome)
- [What Is Gut Dysbiosis?, Cleveland Clinic](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/dysbiosis)
- [How Your Gut Health Affects Your Whole Body, WebMD](https://www.webmd.com/digestive-disorders/ss/slideshow-how-gut-health-affects-whole-body)
- [8 Surprising Things That Harm Your Gut Bacteria, Healthline](https://www.healthline.com/nutrition/8-things-that-harm-gut-bacteria)
